'''Shinagawa Shrine''' (品川神社, Shinagawa-jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Kitashinagawa, Shinagawa-ku, Tokyo. It is one of the {{ill|Tokyo Jissha|ja|東京十社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Jissha|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}} and was historically designated as a {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}. It is also part of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Tōkai Shichifukujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} pilgrimage, enshrining [[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]].

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|ja|天比理刀咩命|1=Amehiratome no Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Amateruhime-no-Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85879124}}
* {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}
* {{ill|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|宇賀之売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

== History ==
The shrine was established in Bunji 3 (1187) by Minamoto no Yoritomo, who transferred the deity Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto from [[:en:Susaki Shrine|Sunosaki Shrine]] in Awa Province for maritime safety and fulfillment of prayers, initially called "Shinagawa Daimyōjin."

In 1319, Nikaido Dōun, a retainer of Hōjō Takatoki, enshrined Uganomitama-no-Mikoto (Inari), and later, in 1478, Ōta Dōkan added Susanoo-no-Mikoto (Tennō).

In 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu visited the shrine before the Battle of Sekigahara, prayed for victory, and later offered masks and mikoshi (portable shrines) in gratitude. Ieyasu granted the shrine a stipend of 5 koku and designated the Tokugawa clan crest (triple hollyhock) as the shrine's emblem. The shrine received considerable support from the Tokugawa shogunate. In 1637, when Tokugawa Iemitsu established {{ill|Tōkai-ji|ja|東海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-ji|1=Tōkai-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527864}}, part of Shinagawa Shrine's grounds became its property, establishing the shrine as its protector.

In 1868, Emperor Meiji designated Shinagawa Shrine as a Jun-Chokusai-sha to protect Tokyo and pray for public peace. It became a Gosha in 1872 and joined the Tokyo Jissha pilgrimage in 1975.

== Grounds ==
{{gallery|width=150|title=Shrine Facilities
|File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii1.jpg|Sōryū Torii at the entrance
|File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii2.jpg|Close-up of descending dragon
|File:ShinagawaJinja_Kagura.jpg|Kagura Hall
|File:Shinagawa Jinja - Sengen Jinja.jpg|Sengen Shrine with Fujizuka
|File:Shinagawa Jinja Fujizuka 02.jpg|Fujizuka (Shinagawa Fuji)}}

; Torii
A "Sōryū Torii" (Twin Dragon Torii) at the shrine's entrance has carved dragons on its pillars: ascending on the left and descending on the right. This torii, along with those at [[:en:Kōenji|Kōenji]] and {{ill|Mabashi Inari Shrine|ja|馬橋稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabashi Inari Shrine|1=Mabashi Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667992}}, is known as one of Tokyo's Three Dragon Torii.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20150926155822/http://www.kyouiku.city.suginami.tokyo.jp/bunkazai/honbun_list.html Cultural Property Board, Suginami Ward Education Committee]</ref>

; Subsidiary Shrines
* Ana Inari Shrine
* Sengen Shrine (adjacent Fujizuka: Shinagawa Fuji)

== Itagaki Taisuke's Grave ==
; Grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke
Behind the main shrine building lies the grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke, originally part of Kōgen-in temple, relocated after the Great Kanto Earthquake. Access is through the shrine grounds, although the grave is not officially part of the shrine.

== Main Festival ==
Held annually from Friday to Sunday closest to June 7.

== Cultural Properties ==
* Tokyo Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property
** Shinagawa Shrine Daidai Kagura{{sfn|Tokyo Metropolitan Government}}
* Shinagawa Ward Designated Cultural Properties
** Stone Torii (Sōryū Torii)
** Stone Basin (Keian 1)
** Stone Lantern
** Mikoshi (Aoi Mikoshi)
** Shinagawa Shrine Documents
** Shinagawa Fuji (largest Fujizuka in Tokyo, approx. 15m)
** Grave of Itagaki Taisuke
** Shinagawa Bayashi

== Access ==
* 3-minute walk from [[:en:Shimbamba Station|Shimbamba Station]] ({{OSM経路図|start=35.61783,139.74117|goal=35.61846,139.73973|name=Route Map}})

|File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii1.jpg|Sōryū Torii at the entrance

|File:Shinagawa Jinja Fujizuka 02.jpg|Fujizuka (Shinagawa Fuji)}}

; Torii
A "Sōryū Torii" (Twin Dragon Torii) at the shrine's entrance has carved dragons on its pillars: ascending on the left and descending on the right. This torii, along with those at [[:en:Kōenji|Kōenji]] and {{ill|Mabashi Inari Shrine|ja|馬橋稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabashi Inari Shrine|1=Mabashi Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667992}}, is known as one of Tokyo's Three Dragon Torii.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20150926155822/http://www.kyouiku.city.suginami.tokyo.jp/bunkazai/honbun_list.html Cultural Property Board, Suginami Ward Education Committee]</ref>

; Subsidiary Shrines
* Ana Inari Shrine
* Sengen Shrine (adjacent Fujizuka: Shinagawa Fuji)

== Itagaki Taisuke's Grave ==
; Grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke
Behind the main shrine building lies the grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke, originally part of Kōgen-in temple, relocated after the Great Kanto Earthquake. Access is through the shrine grounds, although the grave is not officially part of the shrine.

== Main Festival ==
Held annually from Friday to Sunday closest to June 7.

== Cultural Properties ==
* Tokyo Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property
** Shinagawa Shrine Daidai Kagura{{sfn|Tokyo Metropolitan Government}}
* Shinagawa Ward Designated Cultural Properties
** Stone Torii (Sōryū Torii)
** Stone Basin (Keian 1)
** Stone Lantern
** Mikoshi (Aoi Mikoshi)
** Shinagawa Shrine Documents
** Shinagawa Fuji (largest Fujizuka in Tokyo, approx. 15m)
** Grave of Itagaki Taisuke
** Shinagawa Bayashi

== Access ==
* 3-minute walk from [[:en:Shimbamba Station|Shimbamba Station]] ({{OSM経路図|start=35.61783,139.74117|goal=35.61846,139.73973|name=Route Map}})

'''Shinagawa Shrine''' (品川神社, Shinagawa-jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Kitashinagawa, Shinagawa-ku, Tokyo. It is one of the {{ill|Tokyo Jissha|ja|東京十社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Jissha|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}} and was historically designated as a {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}. It is also part of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Tōkai Shichifukujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} pilgrimage, enshrining [[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]].

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|ja|天比理刀咩命|1=Amehiratome no Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Amateruhime-no-Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85879124}}
* {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}
* {{ill|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|宇賀之売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

== History ==
The shrine was established in Bunji 3 (1187) by Minamoto no Yoritomo, who transferred the deity Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto from [[:en:Susaki Shrine|Sunosaki Shrine]] in Awa Province for maritime safety and fulfillment of prayers, initially called "Shinagawa Daimyōjin."

In 1319, Nikaido Dōun, a retainer of Hōjō Takatoki, enshrined Uganomitama-no-Mikoto (Inari), and later, in 1478, Ōta Dōkan added Susanoo-no-Mikoto (Tennō).

In 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu visited the shrine before the Battle of Sekigahara, prayed for victory, and later offered masks and mikoshi (portable shrines) in gratitude. Ieyasu granted the shrine a stipend of 5 koku and designated the Tokugawa clan crest (triple hollyhock) as the shrine's emblem. The shrine received considerable support from the Tokugawa shogunate. In 1637, when Tokugawa Iemitsu established {{ill|Tōkai-ji|ja|東海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-ji|1=Tōkai-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527864}}, part of Shinagawa Shrine's grounds became its property, establishing the shrine as its protector.

In 1868, Emperor Meiji designated Shinagawa Shrine as a Jun-Chokusai-sha to protect Tokyo and pray for public peace. It became a Gosha in 1872 and joined the Tokyo Jissha pilgrimage in 1975.

It is one of the {{ill|Tokyo Jissha|ja|東京十社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Jissha|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}} and was historically designated as a {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}. It is also part of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Tōkai Shichifukujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} pilgrimage, enshrining [[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|ja|天比理刀咩命|1=Amehiratome no Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Amateruhime-no-Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85879124}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|宇賀之売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== History == The shrine was established in Bunji 3 (1187) by Minamoto no Yoritomo, who transferred the deity Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto from [[:en:Susaki Shrine|Sunosaki Shrine]] in Awa Province for maritime safety and fulfillment of prayers, initially called "Shinagawa Daimyōjin."
In 1319, Nikaido Dōun, a retainer of Hōjō Takatoki, enshrined Uganomitama-no-Mikoto (Inari), and later, in 1478, Ōta Dōkan added Susanoo-no-Mikoto (Tennō).
In 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu visited the shrine before the Battle of Sekigahara, prayed for victory, and later offered masks and mikoshi (portable shrines) in gratitude. Ieyasu granted the shrine a stipend of 5 koku and designated the Tokugawa clan crest (triple hollyhock) as the shrine's emblem. The shrine received considerable support from the Tokugawa shogunate. In 1637, when Tokugawa Iemitsu established {{ill|Tōkai-ji|ja|東海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-ji|1=Tōkai-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527864}}, part of Shinagawa Shrine's grounds became its property, establishing the shrine as its protector.
In 1868, Emperor Meiji designated Shinagawa Shrine as a Jun-Chokusai-sha to protect Tokyo and pray for public peace. It became a Gosha in 1872 and joined the Tokyo Jissha pilgrimage in 1975.
== Grounds == {{gallery|width=150|title=Shrine Facilities |File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii1.jpg|Sōryū Torii at the entrance |File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii2.jpg|Close-up of descending dragon |File:ShinagawaJinja_Kagura.jpg|Kagura Hall |File:Shinagawa Jinja - Sengen Jinja.jpg|Sengen Shrine with Fujizuka |File:Shinagawa Jinja Fujizuka 02.jpg|Fujizuka (Shinagawa Fuji)}}
; Torii A "Sōryū Torii" (Twin Dragon Torii) at the shrine's entrance has carved dragons on its pillars: ascending on the left and descending on the right. This torii, along with those at [[:en:Kōenji|Kōenji]] and {{ill|Mabashi Inari Shrine|ja|馬橋稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabashi Inari Shrine|1=Mabashi Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667992}}, is known as one of Tokyo's Three Dragon Torii.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20150926155822/http://www.kyouiku.city.suginami.tokyo.jp/bunkazai/honbun_list.html Cultural Property Board, Suginami Ward Education Committee]</ref>
; Subsidiary Shrines * Ana Inari Shrine * Sengen Shrine (adjacent Fujizuka: Shinagawa Fuji)
== Itagaki Taisuke's Grave == ; Grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke Behind the main shrine building lies the grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke, originally part of Kōgen-in temple, relocated after the Great Kanto Earthquake. Access is through the shrine grounds, although the grave is not officially part of the shrine.
== Main Festival == Held annually from Friday to Sunday closest to June 7.
== Cultural Properties == * Tokyo Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property ** Shinagawa Shrine Daidai Kagura{{sfn|Tokyo Metropolitan Government}} * Shinagawa Ward Designated Cultural Properties ** Stone Torii (Sōryū Torii) ** Stone Basin (Keian 1) ** Stone Lantern ** Mikoshi (Aoi Mikoshi) ** Shinagawa Shrine Documents ** Shinagawa Fuji (largest Fujizuka in Tokyo, approx. 15m) ** Grave of Itagaki Taisuke ** Shinagawa Bayashi
== Access == * 3-minute walk from [[:en:Shimbamba Station|Shimbamba Station]] ({{OSM経路図|start=35.61783,139.74117|goal=35.61846,139.73973|name=Route Map}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shinagawa-jinja]][[ja:品川神社]][[pl:Shinagawa-jinja]][[zh:品川神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shinagawa-jinja]][[ja:品川神社]][[pl:Shinagawa-jinja]][[zh:品川神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinagawa-jinja]][[ja:品川神社]][[pl:Shinagawa-jinja]][[zh:品川神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11418419]] Wikidata name: Shinagawa Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building |name = Shinagawa Shrine |image = File:ShinagawaJinja Honden.jpg |location = 3-7-15 Kitashinagawa, Shinagawa-ku, Tokyo{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35.618472|N|139.739639|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Q85879124|WD=Q85879124}}; {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}} |Rank = Former {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}, Former Gō-sha |established = Bunji 3 (1187){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} |honden = Nagare-zukuri |pilgrimage = {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Tōkai Shichifukujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} |festival = Weekend nearest to June 7 |map= Tokyo city |prefecture= {{ill|Kita-Shinagawa|WD=Q11401302}} |founded_by= {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800}} |part_of= {{ill|Q28685055|WD=Q28685055}}; {{ill|Tokyo-jissha|WD=Q164895}} }}
'''Shinagawa Shrine''' (品川神社, Shinagawa-jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Kitashinagawa, Shinagawa-ku, Tokyo. It is one of the {{ill|Tokyo Jissha|ja|東京十社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Jissha|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}} and was historically designated as a {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}. It is also part of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Tōkai Shichifukujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} pilgrimage, enshrining {{ill|Daikokuten|en|Daikokuten|WD=Q1759916|lt=Daikokuten}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|ja|天比理刀咩命|1=Amehiratome no Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Amateruhime-no-Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85879124}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|宇賀之売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== History == The shrine was established in Bunji 3 (1187) by Minamoto no Yoritomo, who transferred the deity Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto from {{ill|Susaki Shrine|en|Susaki Shrine|WD=Q11556513|lt=Sunosaki Shrine}} in Awa Province for maritime safety and fulfillment of prayers, initially called "Shinagawa Daimyōjin."
In 1319, Nikaido Dōun, a retainer of Hōjō Takatoki, enshrined Uganomitama-no-Mikoto (Inari), and later, in 1478, Ōta Dōkan added Susanoo-no-Mikoto (Tennō).
In 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu visited the shrine before the Battle of Sekigahara, prayed for victory, and later offered masks and mikoshi (portable shrines) in gratitude. Ieyasu granted the shrine a stipend of 5 koku and designated the Tokugawa clan crest (triple hollyhock) as the shrine's emblem. The shrine received considerable support from the Tokugawa shogunate. In 1637, when Tokugawa Iemitsu established {{ill|Tōkai-ji|ja|東海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-ji|1=Tōkai-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527864}}, part of Shinagawa Shrine's grounds became its property, establishing the shrine as its protector.
In 1868, Emperor Meiji designated Shinagawa Shrine as a Jun-Chokusai-sha to protect Tokyo and pray for public peace. It became a Gosha in 1872 and joined the Tokyo Jissha pilgrimage in 1975.
== Grounds == {{gallery|width=150|title=Shrine Facilities |File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii1.jpg|Sōryū Torii at the entrance |File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii2.jpg|Close-up of descending dragon |File:ShinagawaJinja_Kagura.jpg|Kagura Hall |File:Shinagawa Jinja - Sengen Jinja.jpg|Sengen Shrine with Fujizuka |File:Shinagawa Jinja Fujizuka 02.jpg|Fujizuka (Shinagawa Fuji)}}
; Torii A "Sōryū Torii" (Twin Dragon Torii) at the shrine's entrance has carved dragons on its pillars: ascending on the left and descending on the right. This torii, along with those at {{ill|Kōenji|en|Kōenji|WD=Q3180833|lt=Kōenji}} and {{ill|Mabashi Inari Shrine|ja|馬橋稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabashi Inari Shrine|1=Mabashi Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667992}}, is known as one of Tokyo's Three Dragon Torii.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20150926155822/http://www.kyouiku.city.suginami.tokyo.jp/bunkazai/honbun_list.html Cultural Property Board, Suginami Ward Education Committee]</ref>
; Subsidiary Shrines * Ana Inari Shrine * Sengen Shrine (adjacent Fujizuka: Shinagawa Fuji)
== Itagaki Taisuke's Grave == ; Grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke Behind the main shrine building lies the grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke, originally part of Kōgen-in temple, relocated after the Great Kanto Earthquake. Access is through the shrine grounds, although the grave is not officially part of the shrine.
== Main Festival == Held annually from Friday to Sunday closest to June 7.
== Cultural Properties == * Tokyo Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property ** Shinagawa Shrine Daidai Kagura{{sfn|Tokyo Metropolitan Government}} * Shinagawa Ward Designated Cultural Properties ** Stone Torii (Sōryū Torii) ** Stone Basin (Keian 1) ** Stone Lantern ** Mikoshi (Aoi Mikoshi) ** Shinagawa Shrine Documents ** Shinagawa Fuji (largest Fujizuka in Tokyo, approx. 15m) ** Grave of Itagaki Taisuke ** Shinagawa Bayashi
== Access == * 3-minute walk from {{ill|Shimbamba Station|en|Shimbamba Station|WD=Q863236|lt=Shimbamba Station}} ({{OSM経路図|start=35.61783,139.74117|goal=35.61846,139.73973|name=Route Map}})
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Shinagawa-jinja}} * [https://shinagawajinja.tokyo/ Shinagawa Shrine Official Website] * {{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2020-7-18|ref=harv}} * [https://www.city.shinagawa.tokyo.jp/PC/sangyo/sangyo-bunkazai/sangyo-bunkazai-shitebunkazai/index.html Shinagawa Ward Cultural Properties] * {{Cite web|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=476|title=Shinagawa Shrine Daidai Kagura|accessdate=2020-7-18|publisher=Tokyo Metropolitan Government|ref=harv}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shinagawa-jinja]][[ja:品川神社]][[pl:Shinagawa-jinja]][[zh:品川神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shinagawa-jinja]][[ja:品川神社]][[pl:Shinagawa-jinja]][[zh:品川神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shinagawa-jinja]][[ja:品川神社]][[pl:Shinagawa-jinja]][[zh:品川神社]]
{{Infobox religious building |name = Shinagawa Shrine |image = File:ShinagawaJinja Honden.jpg |location = 3-7-15 Kitashinagawa, Shinagawa-ku, Tokyo{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35.618472|N|139.739639|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Q85879124|WD=Q85879124}}; {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}} |Rank = Former {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}, Former Gō-sha |established = Bunji 3 (1187){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} |honden = Nagare-zukuri |pilgrimage = {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Tōkai Shichifukujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} |festival = Weekend nearest to June 7 |map= Tokyo city |prefecture= {{ill|Kita-Shinagawa|WD=Q11401302}} |founded_by= {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800}} |part_of= {{ill|Q28685055|WD=Q28685055}}; {{ill|Tokyo-jissha|WD=Q164895}} }}
'''Shinagawa Shrine''' (品川神社, Shinagawa-jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Kitashinagawa, Shinagawa-ku, Tokyo. It is one of the {{ill|Tokyo Jissha|ja|東京十社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Jissha|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}} and was historically designated as a {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}. It is also part of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Tōkai Shichifukujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} pilgrimage, enshrining {{ill|Daikokuten|en|Daikokuten|WD=Q1759916|lt=Daikokuten}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|ja|天比理刀咩命|1=Amehiratome no Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Amateruhime-no-Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85879124}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|宇賀之売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== History == The shrine was established in Bunji 3 (1187) by Minamoto no Yoritomo, who transferred the deity Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto from {{ill|Susaki Shrine|en|Susaki Shrine|WD=Q11556513|lt=Sunosaki Shrine}} in Awa Province for maritime safety and fulfillment of prayers, initially called "Shinagawa Daimyōjin."
In 1319, Nikaido Dōun, a retainer of Hōjō Takatoki, enshrined Uganomitama-no-Mikoto (Inari), and later, in 1478, Ōta Dōkan added Susanoo-no-Mikoto (Tennō).
In 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu visited the shrine before the Battle of Sekigahara, prayed for victory, and later offered masks and mikoshi (portable shrines) in gratitude. Ieyasu granted the shrine a stipend of 5 koku and designated the Tokugawa clan crest (triple hollyhock) as the shrine's emblem. The shrine received considerable support from the Tokugawa shogunate. In 1637, when Tokugawa Iemitsu established {{ill|Tōkai-ji|ja|東海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-ji|1=Tōkai-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527864}}, part of Shinagawa Shrine's grounds became its property, establishing the shrine as its protector.
In 1868, Emperor Meiji designated Shinagawa Shrine as a Jun-Chokusai-sha to protect Tokyo and pray for public peace. It became a Gosha in 1872 and joined the Tokyo Jissha pilgrimage in 1975.
== Grounds == {{gallery|width=150|title=Shrine Facilities |File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii1.jpg|Sōryū Torii at the entrance |File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii2.jpg|Close-up of descending dragon |File:ShinagawaJinja_Kagura.jpg|Kagura Hall |File:Shinagawa Jinja - Sengen Jinja.jpg|Sengen Shrine with Fujizuka |File:Shinagawa Jinja Fujizuka 02.jpg|Fujizuka (Shinagawa Fuji)}}
; Torii A "Sōryū Torii" (Twin Dragon Torii) at the shrine's entrance has carved dragons on its pillars: ascending on the left and descending on the right. This torii, along with those at {{ill|Kōenji|en|Kōenji|WD=Q3180833|lt=Kōenji}} and {{ill|Mabashi Inari Shrine|ja|馬橋稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabashi Inari Shrine|1=Mabashi Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667992}}, is known as one of Tokyo's Three Dragon Torii.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20150926155822/http://www.kyouiku.city.suginami.tokyo.jp/bunkazai/honbun_list.html Cultural Property Board, Suginami Ward Education Committee]</ref>
; Subsidiary Shrines * Ana Inari Shrine * Sengen Shrine (adjacent Fujizuka: Shinagawa Fuji)
== Itagaki Taisuke's Grave == ; Grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke Behind the main shrine building lies the grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke, originally part of Kōgen-in temple, relocated after the Great Kanto Earthquake. Access is through the shrine grounds, although the grave is not officially part of the shrine.
== Main Festival == Held annually from Friday to Sunday closest to June 7.
== Cultural Properties == * Tokyo Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property ** Shinagawa Shrine Daidai Kagura{{sfn|Tokyo Metropolitan Government}} * Shinagawa Ward Designated Cultural Properties ** Stone Torii (Sōryū Torii) ** Stone Basin (Keian 1) ** Stone Lantern ** Mikoshi (Aoi Mikoshi) ** Shinagawa Shrine Documents ** Shinagawa Fuji (largest Fujizuka in Tokyo, approx. 15m) ** Grave of Itagaki Taisuke ** Shinagawa Bayashi
== Access == * 3-minute walk from {{ill|Shimbamba Station|en|Shimbamba Station|WD=Q863236|lt=Shimbamba Station}} ({{OSM経路図|start=35.61783,139.74117|goal=35.61846,139.73973|name=Route Map}})
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Shinagawa-jinja}} * [https://shinagawajinja.tokyo/ Shinagawa Shrine Official Website] * {{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2020-7-18|ref=harv}} * [https://www.city.shinagawa.tokyo.jp/PC/sangyo/sangyo-bunkazai/sangyo-bunkazai-shitebunkazai/index.html Shinagawa Ward Cultural Properties] * {{Cite web|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=476|title=Shinagawa Shrine Daidai Kagura|accessdate=2020-7-18|publisher=Tokyo Metropolitan Government|ref=harv}}

This torii, along with those at {{ill|Kōenji|en|Kōenji|WD=Q3180833|lt=Kōenji|1=Kōenji}} and {{ill|Mabashi Inari Shrine|ja|馬橋稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabashi Inari Shrine|1=Mabashi Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667992}}, is known as one of Tokyo's Three Dragon Torii.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20150926155822/http://www.kyouiku.city.suginami.tokyo.jp/bunkazai/honbun_list.html Cultural Property Board, Suginami Ward Education Committee]</ref>
; Subsidiary Shrines * Ana Inari Shrine * Sengen Shrine (adjacent Fujizuka: Shinagawa Fuji)
== Itagaki Taisuke's Grave == ; Grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke Behind the main shrine building lies the grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke, originally part of Kōgen-in temple, relocated after the Great Kanto Earthquake. Access is through the shrine grounds, although the grave is not officially part of the shrine.
== Main Festival == Held annually from Friday to Sunday closest to June 7.
== Cultural Properties == * Tokyo Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property ** Shinagawa Shrine Daidai Kagura{{sfn|Tokyo Metropolitan Government}} * Shinagawa Ward Designated Cultural Properties ** Stone Torii (Sōryū Torii) ** Stone Basin (Keian 1) ** Stone Lantern ** Mikoshi (Aoi Mikoshi) ** Shinagawa Shrine Documents ** Shinagawa Fuji (largest Fujizuka in Tokyo, approx. 15m) ** Grave of Itagaki Taisuke ** Shinagawa Bayashi
== Access == * 3-minute walk from {{ill|Shimbamba Station|en|Shimbamba Station|WD=Q863236|lt=Shimbamba Station|1=Shimbamba Station}} ({{OSM経路図|start=35.61783,139.74117|goal=35.61846,139.73973|name=Route Map}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shinagawa-jinja]][[ja:品川神社]][[pl:Shinagawa-jinja]][[zh:品川神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shinagawa-jinja]][[ja:品川神社]][[pl:Shinagawa-jinja]][[zh:品川神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Shinagawa-jinja]][[ja:品川神社]][[pl:Shinagawa-jinja]][[zh:品川神社]]
{{Infobox religious building |name = Shinagawa Shrine |image = File:ShinagawaJinja Honden.jpg |location = 3-7-15 Kitashinagawa, Shinagawa-ku, Tokyo{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35.618472|N|139.739639|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Q85879124|WD=Q85879124|1=Ame no Hiratome}}; {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728|1=Ukanomitama}} |Rank = Former {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}, Former Gō-sha |established = Bunji 3 (1187){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Agency}} |honden = Nagare-zukuri |pilgrimage = {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Q28685055|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} |festival = Weekend nearest to June 7 |map= Tokyo city |prefecture= {{ill|Kita-Shinagawa|WD=Q11401302|1=Kita-Shinagawa}} |founded_by= {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} |part_of= {{ill|Q28685055|WD=Q28685055|1=Q28685055}}; {{ill|Tokyo-jissha|WD=Q164895|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines}} }}
'''Shinagawa Shrine''' (品川神社, Shinagawa-jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Kitashinagawa, Shinagawa-ku, Tokyo. It is one of the {{ill|Tokyo Jissha|ja|東京十社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Jissha|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}} and was historically designated as a {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}. It is also part of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Q28685055|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} pilgrimage, enshrining {{ill|Daikokuten|en|Daikokuten|WD=Q1759916|lt=Daikokuten|1=Daikokuten}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|ja|天比理刀咩命|1=Ame no Hiratome|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Amateruhime-no-Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85879124}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|宇賀之売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== History == The shrine was established in Bunji 3 (1187) by Minamoto no Yoritomo, who transferred the deity Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto from {{ill|Susaki Shrine|en|Susaki Shrine|WD=Q11556513|lt=Sunosaki Shrine|1=Susaki Shrine}} in Awa Province for maritime safety and fulfillment of prayers, initially called "Shinagawa Daimyōjin."
In 1319, Nikaido Dōun, a retainer of Hōjō Takatoki, enshrined Uganomitama-no-Mikoto (Inari), and later, in 1478, Ōta Dōkan added Susanoo-no-Mikoto (Tennō).
In 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu visited the shrine before the Battle of Sekigahara, prayed for victory, and later offered masks and mikoshi (portable shrines) in gratitude. Ieyasu granted the shrine a stipend of 5 koku and designated the Tokugawa clan crest (triple hollyhock) as the shrine's emblem. The shrine received considerable support from the Tokugawa shogunate. In 1637, when Tokugawa Iemitsu established {{ill|Tōkai-ji|ja|東海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-ji|1=Q11527864|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527864}}, part of Shinagawa Shrine's grounds became its property, establishing the shrine as its protector.
In 1868, Emperor Meiji designated Shinagawa Shrine as a Jun-Chokusai-sha to protect Tokyo and pray for public peace. It became a Gosha in 1872 and joined the Tokyo Jissha pilgrimage in 1975.
== Grounds == {{gallery|width=150|title=Shrine Facilities |File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii1.jpg|Sōryū Torii at the entrance |File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii2.jpg|Close-up of descending dragon |File:ShinagawaJinja_Kagura.jpg|Kagura Hall |File:Shinagawa Jinja - Sengen Jinja.jpg|Sengen Shrine with Fujizuka |File:Shinagawa Jinja Fujizuka 02.jpg|Fujizuka (Shinagawa Fuji)}}
; Torii A "Sōryū Torii" (Twin Dragon Torii) at the shrine's entrance has carved dragons on its pillars: ascending on the left and descending on the right. This torii, along with those at {{ill|Kōenji|en|Kōenji|WD=Q3180833|lt=Kōenji|1=Kōenji}} and {{ill|Mabashi Inari Shrine|ja|馬橋稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabashi Inari Shrine|1=Mabashi Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667992}}, is known as one of Tokyo's Three Dragon Torii.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20150926155822/http://www.kyouiku.city.suginami.tokyo.jp/bunkazai/honbun_list.html Cultural Property Board, Suginami Ward Education Committee]</ref>
; Subsidiary Shrines * Ana Inari Shrine * Sengen Shrine (adjacent Fujizuka: Shinagawa Fuji)
== Itagaki Taisuke's Grave == ; Grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke Behind the main shrine building lies the grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke, originally part of Kōgen-in temple, relocated after the Great Kanto Earthquake. Access is through the shrine grounds, although the grave is not officially part of the shrine.
== Main Festival == Held annually from Friday to Sunday closest to June 7.
== Cultural Properties == * Tokyo Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property ** Shinagawa Shrine Daidai Kagura{{sfn|Tokyo Metropolitan Government}} * Shinagawa Ward Designated Cultural Properties ** Stone Torii (Sōryū Torii) ** Stone Basin (Keian 1) ** Stone Lantern ** Mikoshi (Aoi Mikoshi) ** Shinagawa Shrine Documents ** Shinagawa Fuji (largest Fujizuka in Tokyo, approx. 15m) ** Grave of Itagaki Taisuke ** Shinagawa Bayashi
== Access == * 3-minute walk from {{ill|Shimbamba Station|en|Shimbamba Station|WD=Q863236|lt=Shimbamba Station|1=Shimbamba Station}} ({{OSM経路図|start=35.61783,139.74117|goal=35.61846,139.73973|name=Route Map}})

It is also part of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Q28685055|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} pilgrimage, enshrining {{ill|Daikokuten|en|Daikokuten|WD=Q1759916|lt=Daikokuten|1=Daikokuten}}.
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|ja|天比理刀咩命|1=Ame no Hiratome|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Amateruhime-no-Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85879124}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja|宇賀之売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uganomitama-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
== History == The shrine was established in Bunji 3 (1187) by Minamoto no Yoritomo, who transferred the deity Amenohirinome-no-Mikoto from {{ill|Susaki Shrine|en|Susaki Shrine|WD=Q11556513|lt=Sunosaki Shrine|1=Susaki Shrine}} in Awa Province for maritime safety and fulfillment of prayers, initially called "Shinagawa Daimyōjin."
In 1319, Nikaido Dōun, a retainer of Hōjō Takatoki, enshrined Uganomitama-no-Mikoto (Inari), and later, in 1478, Ōta Dōkan added Susanoo-no-Mikoto (Tennō).
In 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu visited the shrine before the Battle of Sekigahara, prayed for victory, and later offered masks and mikoshi (portable shrines) in gratitude. Ieyasu granted the shrine a stipend of 5 koku and designated the Tokugawa clan crest (triple hollyhock) as the shrine's emblem. The shrine received considerable support from the Tokugawa shogunate. In 1637, when Tokugawa Iemitsu established {{ill|Tōkai-ji|ja|東海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai-ji|1=Q11527864|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527864}}, part of Shinagawa Shrine's grounds became its property, establishing the shrine as its protector.
In 1868, Emperor Meiji designated Shinagawa Shrine as a Jun-Chokusai-sha to protect Tokyo and pray for public peace. It became a Gosha in 1872 and joined the Tokyo Jissha pilgrimage in 1975.
== Grounds == {{gallery|width=150|title=Shrine Facilities |File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii1.jpg|Sōryū Torii at the entrance |File:ShinagawaJinja_SoryuTorii2.jpg|Close-up of descending dragon |File:ShinagawaJinja_Kagura.jpg|Kagura Hall |File:Shinagawa Jinja - Sengen Jinja.jpg|Sengen Shrine with Fujizuka |File:Shinagawa Jinja Fujizuka 02.jpg|Fujizuka (Shinagawa Fuji)}}
; Torii A "Sōryū Torii" (Twin Dragon Torii) at the shrine's entrance has carved dragons on its pillars: ascending on the left and descending on the right. This torii, along with those at {{ill|Kōenji|en|Kōenji|WD=Q3180833|lt=Kōenji|1=Kōenji}} and {{ill|Mabashi Inari Shrine|ja|馬橋稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mabashi Inari Shrine|1=Mabashi Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11667992}}, is known as one of Tokyo's Three Dragon Torii.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20150926155822/http://www.kyouiku.city.suginami.tokyo.jp/bunkazai/honbun_list.html Cultural Property Board, Suginami Ward Education Committee]</ref>
; Subsidiary Shrines * Ana Inari Shrine * Sengen Shrine (adjacent Fujizuka: Shinagawa Fuji)
== Itagaki Taisuke's Grave == ; Grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke Behind the main shrine building lies the grave of Count Itagaki Taisuke, originally part of Kōgen-in temple, relocated after the Great Kanto Earthquake. Access is through the shrine grounds, although the grave is not officially part of the shrine.
== Main Festival == Held annually from Friday to Sunday closest to June 7.
== Cultural Properties == * Tokyo Designated Intangible Folk Cultural Property ** Shinagawa Shrine Daidai Kagura{{sfn|Tokyo Metropolitan Government}} * Shinagawa Ward Designated Cultural Properties ** Stone Torii (Sōryū Torii) ** Stone Basin (Keian 1) ** Stone Lantern ** Mikoshi (Aoi Mikoshi) ** Shinagawa Shrine Documents ** Shinagawa Fuji (largest Fujizuka in Tokyo, approx. 15m) ** Grave of Itagaki Taisuke ** Shinagawa Bayashi
